coredata.dc:title: 1,10-Phenanthroline-5-amine derived N-doped carbon dots for long-lived visible-light-activated room temperature phosphorescence in the matrix and information encryption application
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Wansong
Hu, Wanchao
Shi, Bingfeng
Lü, Changli
coredata.dc:description: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have received a lot of attention. However, it is still a challenge to realize the practical application of RTP excited under visible light. Herein, five different carbon dots (CDs) were prepared using 1,10-phenanthroline-5-amine (Aphen) as the co-carbon source and nitrogen source, and several polymer-based RTP films were prepared by simple doping. It was found that the phosphorescence emission behavior of polymer films was changed by the surface state structure of different CDs. Non-radiative transition of the triplet excitons of Aphen structure on the CDs surface was effectively inhibited by the hydrogen bonding interaction and the fixed domain-limiting effect of the polymer matrix. Meanwhile, the Aphen structure was bound to the CDs through covalent interaction, which further suppressed the molecular vibration, realizing the efficient organic RTP. The phosphorescence lifetime of the prepared ACCDs-PVA films reached 352.88 ms with a photoluminescence quantum yield of 36.7%. In addition, the dye RhB was co-doped in the ACCDs-PVA system to modulate the afterglow color of the materials via the triplet to single-state energy resonance transfer (TS-FRET) process. Finally, we also developed stable ACCDs-EPO RTP material by exploiting the dense three-dimensional network structure and hydrophobic property of the cured epoxy resin (EPO) matrix. The as-constructed long-lived RTP materials can be employed in information encryption.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication of CDs-doped different matrices with hydrogen or covalent bonding induced RTP and the mechanism of photophysical processes.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of different CDs and their photographs in ethanol solution (0.1 mg/mL) under daylight (left) and 365 nm irradiation (right).

TEM images and size distributions of (a) ACCDs, (e) ACCDs-1 and (i) ACCDs-2. High-resolution XPS spectra of (b, f, j) C 1s peaks, (c, g, k) N 1s peaks and (d, h, l) O 1s peaks of ACCDs, ACCDs-1 and ACCDs-2.

Photophysical properties of PVA films containing various concentrations of ACCDs (1:9): (a) fluorescence spectra, (b) RTP spectra (λex = 405 nm), (c) time-resolved phosphorescence decay curves (λex = 280 nm), and (d) optical photographs before and after the 405 nm excitation lamp is turned off.

Tunable afterglow color is achieved by TS-FRET: (a) Jablonski schematic diagram for TS-FRET process; (b) Fluorescence and (c) RTP spectra of ACCDs-PVA films containing various RhB concentrations; (d) Photographs of ACCDs-RhB-PVA with different RhB doping concentrations taken under 405 nm lamp.

(a) Flexible films prepared by ACCDs-PVA and CDs-A-PVA; (b) Information encryption application of ACCDs-BA; (c) Water resistance of ACCDs-EPO under turn-on and -off with 405 nm excitation light source after 10 days.

head.ce:title: 1,10-Phenanthroline-5-amine derived N-doped carbon dots for long-lived visible-light-activated room temperature phosphorescence in the matrix and information encryption application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun, 130024, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun, 130024, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: W. S. Sun and W. C. Hu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have received a lot of attention. However, it is still a challenge to realize the practical application of RTP excited under visible light. Herein, five different carbon dots (CDs) were prepared using 1,10-phenanthroline-5-amine (Aphen) as the co-carbon source and nitrogen source, and several polymer-based RTP films were prepared by simple doping. It was found that the phosphorescence emission behavior of polymer films was changed by the surface state structure of different CDs. Non-radiative transition of the triplet excitons of Aphen structure on the CDs surface was effectively inhibited by the hydrogen bonding interaction and the fixed domain-limiting effect of the polymer matrix. Meanwhile, the Aphen structure was bound to the CDs through covalent interaction, which further suppressed the molecular vibration, realizing the efficient organic RTP. The phosphorescence lifetime of the prepared ACCDs-PVA films reached 352.88 ms with a photoluminescence quantum yield of 36.7%. In addition, the dye RhB was co-doped in the ACCDs-PVA system to modulate the afterglow color of the materials via the triplet to single-state energy resonance transfer (TS-FRET) process. Finally, we also developed stable ACCDs-EPO RTP material by exploiting the dense three-dimensional network structure and hydrophobic property of the cured epoxy resin (EPO) matrix. The as-constructed long-lived RTP materials can be employed in information encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:sections.ce:para: Visible-light excited long-lived Aphen-derived CDs-based RTP materials are achieved via suppressing the vibration of Aphen moiety covalently binded to CDs in the matrix. The afterglow color can be adjusted to red by a TS-FRET process. The water stability of phosphorescence is also improved by introducing ACCDs in cured hydrophobic EPO matrix. The as-constructed long-lived RTP materials have great potential in information encryption application.

ce:section.ce:para: Phosphorescence is a delayed luminescent phenomenon accompanied by a long-lived excited triple state [1]. In recent years, the functional materials with room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) have aroused widespread concern in the fields of sensing, information encryption and biological imaging due to their long afterglow life, large Stokes shift and excellent characteristics [2–6]. Theoretically, the existence of effective intersystem crossover (ISC) from singlet (S1) to triplet (Tn) and the persistence of a stable triplet excitons generated by the non-radiative transitions are the two keys for designing RTP materials [7–9]. However, it is difficult to obtain phosphorescence originating from the triplet exciton transition spin inhibition in RTP materials. So far, a series of significant advancements in the preparation of RTP materials have been made, but currently, the main RTP materials reported are rare earth compounds [10–13], organometallic complex [14–16], and organic crystalline [17–19]. However, the above RTP materials have the imperfections such as excessive cost of materials, complicated preparation processes, great toxicity, short afterglow lifetimes, and low quantum yields, constraining their practical applications in numerous areas. As a consequence, the development of low-cost, low-toxicity, long-lived emission materials that can be viewed by naked eyes is an urgent requirement for achieving efficient RTP [6,20–22].

Carbon dots (CDs), as the new star of light-emitting carbon nanomaterials, are considered as the promising non-heavy atomic materials for realizing RTP due to their unique carbon skeleton structure and abundant surface functional groups. Thanks to inexpensive sources, convenient preparation, low cost and toxicity, and adjustable distinguished optoelectronic performance, CDs have extremely high application value in light-emitting diodes, chemical sensors, bio-imaging and security encryption [4,23–27]. Compared with conventional organic molecules, CDs have wider emission peaks and larger Stokes shifts [2,28,29]. Due to molecular vibration dissipation and oxygen quenching of triplet excitons, they are generally difficult to observe phosphorescence of CDs at room temperature [79–81]. Therefore, in order to obtain effective RTP emission, two aspects should be considered. On the one hand, the effective ISC from the lowest S1 to Tn is populated with a sufficient number of triplet state excitons. On the other hand, the triplet excitons are stabilized by reducing the non-radiative attenuation and quenching process of T1 [23,30–32].

Based on these advantages, a few of strategies have been developed to make RTP materials with glorious properties utilizing different CDs, including the introduction of heavy atoms and the embedding of CDs in various matrices. Particularly, matrix-assisted means are extensively applied in recent years. The as-employed matrices include organic materials (e. g., polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [33–35], polyurethane (PU) [36], polyacrylamide (PAM) [37], polyethylene pyrrolidone (PVP) [38], urea/biurea [29], melamine [26,39] and metal-organic framework (MOF) [40]) and inorganic substances (e. g., aluminum sulfate [41], zeolite [42], silica [43–45], boric acid [46,47], two-dimensional layered bimetallic hydroxide (LDH) [48,49] and alkali soil carbonates [50,51]). As CDs are imbedded in a rigid substrate, their triplet state exciton can be protected and activated by confinement effects (such as hydrogen bonding, covalent bonding and three-dimensional space confinement) to produce efficient RTP emission. For example, Hu et al. firstly reported an efficient method mediated by energy transfer that greatly enhanced the phosphorescence by embedding CDs in different afterglow matrices [41]. In the CDs embedded in boric acid or urea composite systems, all the prepared materials can emit RTP visible to the naked eye for more than 20 s, with N-CDs@B2O3 emitting phosphorescence for up to 30 s. Meanwhile, advanced information decryption and fast fingerprint detection applications are designed based on the prominent feature.

The phenanthroline derivatives take on a large π-conjugate plane structure and better N atom coordination, which can be used as fluorescence materials, but their phosphorescence materials have rarely been reported [74–76]. In our previous work, a simple method was exploited for achieving visible-light activated RTP via embedding 1,10-phenanthroline-5-amine (Aphen) into PVA matrix [64]. Inspired by this, we prepared CDs by Aphen molecules and other carbon sources, hoping to further regulate RTP emitting wavelength, enhancing phosphorescence lifetime and improving luminescence quantum yield. Herein, we proposed a general tactics to prepare visible light-driven CDs-based composite phosphorescence materials. Five different CDs were designed by utilizing Aphen as co-carbon source and nitrogen source. The relationship between different CDs structures and phosphorescence properties was discussed in detail, and the RTP properties of citric acid (CA) and Aphen-derived multiple-cores@shell clustered CDs (ACCDs) doped into polymers and boric acid (BA) were investigated. The phosphorescence lifetime of ACCDs-PVA films can reach 352.9 ms, and the photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) is up to 36.7%. The obtained ACCDs-BA composite exhibited an ultra-long RTP phenomenon, which is about 13 s by the naked eye with a phosphorescence lifetime of 965.3 ms. The mechanism studies showed that the abundant CO/C–N groups on CDs facilitated ISC and effectually suppressed the non-radiative transition of triplet excitons in the Aphen moieties, using hydrogen bonding interactions and fixed domain-limiting interactions between ACCDs and PVA matrix. At the same time, the Aphen structure binds to the CDs through covalent interaction, further inhibits its molecular vibration and achieves efficient organic RTP (Fig. 1 ). In addition, the fluorescence dye rhodamine B (RhB) was used as an energy acceptor, due to the effective triplet to singlet Förster energy resonance transfer (TS-FRET) process, which successfully generates a CDs-based composite film based on emission that is from yellow to red, enabling the possibility of color tuning. Moreover, we have developed epoxy resin-based RTP materials, in which ACCDs are encapsulated to isolate water and oxygen, making the RTP of the system more stable and ensuring phosphorescence in the aqueous system. Based on the superior phosphorescence properties, we have successfully applied it to information encryption.

CA (2.7 g) and Aphen (0.3 g) were put into 30 mL deionized water in polytetrafluoride hydrothermal reactor, which is subjected to hydrothermal reaction for 7 h at 200 °C. After the temperature of hydrothermal vessel was naturally decreased to 20 °C, the reaction solution was filtered by utilizing a microporous film (0.22 μm), and then dialyzed in a dialysis bag (interception molecular weight: 1000 Da) for 48 h. The resulted product was cleaned with deionized water before centrifuged for 10 min at 10000 rpm twice in a row. Finally, the ACCDs solid powder was acquired by lyophilization. In addition, we also used the similar reaction process above to change the mass of CA. The mass ratio of Aphen/CA was also changed to 1:5 and 1:15 (0.75 g and 2.25 g for CA, respectively), and ACCDs were synthesized under these conditions [52].
Aphen (0.3 g) and CA (2.7 g) were put in anhydrous ethanol (30 mL) for ACCDs-1 or DMF (30 mL) for ACCDs-2, and the other reactions were the same as the preparation of ACCDs.

Aphen (0.30 g) and glucose (2.7 g) were added to deionized water (30 mL). 15 μL of phosphoric acid was added to aid solubilization. The rest of the reaction procedures were the same as the preparation of ACCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of pure citric acid CDs (CACDs)
ce:section.ce:para: CA (4.0 g) in a reaction flask was heated for continuous 20 min at 225 °C using an electric heating sleeve. After the sample was cooled, distilled water (10 mL) was put to dissolve the solid, and 1.0 M NaOH solution was put dropwise to bring the pH value of the solution to neutral. Finally, the above solution was dialyzed for 36 h in the dialysis bag (1 KDa), and solid product was obtained by freeze-drying.
CACDs (2.7 g) and Aphen (0.3 g) were put in 30 mL deionized water, and the other reactions were the same as the preparation of ACCDs.

Various amounts of ACCDs dissolved in 0.5 mL ethanol were mixed with the aqueous solution of PVA (40 mg/mL) respectively and ultrasound was performed for 30 min. The resulting mixtures were cast on a glass of 6 × 6 cm2 and vacuum-dried for 10 h at 60 °C to gain a transparent membrane. According to the proportion (mg/mL) of Aphen in PVA solution, the resulting polymer films were defined as ACCDs-PVA-0.05, ACCDs-PVA-0.1 and ACCDs-PVA-0.5, respectively. Other doped film samples were also attained via simply changing the polymer matrices (PMMA or PS) in the same way as above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of TS-FRET polymer composite films
ce:section.ce:para: A certain mass of ACCDS-PVA solution was taken, and RhB was added according to the mass ratio (5, 10 and 20 wt %) of RhB/ACCDs in PVA films. Other operations after ultrasound were the same as above.

2 mg ACCDs was added to 40 mL deionized water with 3 g BA, and ultrasonic stirring was conducted for 10 min. The reaction system was agitated for 5 h at 180 °C and then ground into powder after cooling to 20 °C [53].

3 g E51 was mixed with 7.5 mg ACCDs and stirred for 1 h. Curing agent (W93) was put into the resin mixture with a 1/3 mass ratio of W93/E51. Then, the resulting mixture was cast on 6 × 6 cm2 glass, left at 25 °C for 2 h, and thermally cured at a vacuum oven at 60, 80, 100, and 120 °C for 1 h respectively, and finally for 0.5 h 150 °C. The as-obtained sample was denoted as ACCDs-EPO-0.1.

Where, ΔEST, λFL and λPhos represent the energy gap between triplet and singlet states, the maximum wavelengths of fluorescence emission and phosphorescent emission, respectively.

As illustrated in Fig. 2 , ACCDs were gained through the one-step hydrothermal treatment by employing Aphen and CA as raw materials [52]. For comparison, ACCDs-1 and ACCDs-2 were prepared by solvent-thermal process using different solvents (ethanol, DMF) under the same conditions as described above. The CDs morphology was measured by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As given in Fig. 3 a, the average size of ACCDs is 70 nm, and the multi-core@shell structure is visible to the naked eye. The HRTEM images exhibit apparent lattice fringes (lattice spacing: 0.35 nm), which is corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite [6]. As seen in Fig. 3e, the particles of ACCDs-1 are uniformly distributed, in which the average particle size is 3.52 nm. According to the results of HRTEM images, the lattice stripes of ACCDs-1 are clearly visible with an interval of 0.22 nm, which is ascribed to the (100) plane of graphite [21]. The size distribution of ACCDs-2 is between 1.3 and 3.7 nm, where the average size is 2.33 nm (Fig. 3i). A clear lattice with fringe spacing of 0.22 nm for the (100) plane of graphite can also be observed in HRTEM. Because Aphen can be well dissolved in ethanol and DMF solutions, it is possible that the uniform reaction leads to the formation of normal-sized CDs during the reaction. In the hydrothermal method, ACCDs is nucleated by CA and then reacts with Aphen, thus ACCDs has a larger size [52].

We also chose AGCDs generated from glucose instead of CA as a control sample. It can be apparently viewed from TEM images (Fig. S1a) that the small AGCDs reveal a particle size distribution of 2.2–4.6 nm with the average diameter of about 3.11 nm. The above results suggest that AGCDs does not form large-sized CDs similar to the core-shell structure of ACCDs [52]. To further demonstrate the effect of the multicore-shell structure of ACCDs on phosphorescence, the structure of CDs-A post-functionalized with Aphen was also studied. As is known, the size of CDs is generally less than 10 nm, but the as-produced ACCDs from Aphen and CA under hydrothermal condition display a larger size of approximately 70 nm. We hold the opinion that ACCDs is formed by core-shell materials obtained by CA and Aphen. Due to the fast reaction rate of CA molecules, small CDs (CACDs) are first formed in the reaction system, then CACDs act as the core to react with Aphen [52]. As shown in Fig. S2, the CACDs can be regarded as uniformly dispersed particles with the average diameter of 3.0 nm. The size of the CDs-A has increased significantly (Fig. S1e), so we believe that Aphen molecules are wrapped around the surface of the CACDs.

The element compositions and surface groups of five CDs were also analyzed by FT-IR and XPS. As shown in Fig. S3, the FT-IR spectra show that ACCDs (black line) have several characteristic absorption bands: 3431, 3220, 3066 and 1580 cm−1, caused from the O–H, N–H, unsaturated C–H and CC stretching vibrations, respectively [2,36,54,55]. Furthermore, IR vibration peaks of C–N = , C–O and Aphen benzene rings in CDs can be observed at 1430, 1230-1180, and 856-806 cm−1. Similarly, the typical vibration bands of Aphen moieties were also observed in the other four CDs. ACCDs, ACCDs-1, and ACCDs-2 display the characteristic peak of the carbonyl groups at around 1700 cm−1 resulted from the amidation reaction of CA and Aphen. The vibrational peak of the amide bond (CONH) is overlapped in this band [21]. In CDs-A (orange line), there are essentially similar IR absorption peaks compared with ACCDs, and the vibration peak of CONH is also observed at 1650-1690 cm−1, proving that –CONH– is formed by the reaction between the carboxyl group on the CACDs and the amino group of Aphen. Therefore, we suggest that the Aphen molecule in the CDs-A is wrapped around the CACDs surface.

Three major signals of C/O/N 1s can be identified in the XPS spectra, confirming that the ACCDs is rich in nitrogen and oxygen groups with the atomic percentages for C, O and N being 68.37%, 2.67% and 28.96%, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. 3b) reveal three typical signals at the binding energy of 284.6 eV, 286.4 eV and 288.5 eV, ascribed to CC/C–C, C–N/C–O and CO. The N 1s peak (Fig. 3c) can be divided into four bands including pyridine N (398.2 eV), amino N (399.2 eV), pyrrole N (400.0 eV) and graphite N (401.5 eV), respectively. In Fig. 3d, the O 1s peak is divided into two bands of CO (532.3 eV) and C–O (534.3 eV) [2,52,56]. The Aphen molecules can be shown to through a slight carbonization during the formation of ACCDs. In addition, the presence of amide bonds can be demonstrated by the above mentioned XPS data.

To further insight into the influence of the solvent reaction system on the CDs structure, XPS analysis was performed on ACCDs-1 and ACCDs-2 (Fig. 3f–h, j-l). The spectra show that the same elements: C/N/O 1s exist in the two as-formed CDs. Furthermore, the contents of N atoms are 6.10% and 11.67% in ACCDs-1 and ACCDs-2, respectively (Table S1). This indicates that the functional groups for the synthesized CDs at different solvothermal reaction conditions changed significantly, and the N atom content is significantly inferior compared with that of ACCDs. Aphen can be completely dissolved in ethanol and DMF, which makes the solubility of CDs obtained by the solvothermal method superior to that of ACCDs during the formation of CDs. Therefore, Aphen moieties is evenly distributed during the formation of CDs, resulting in a low assay of N atoms and high assay of O atoms in the surface states of CDs. However, the surface of ACCDs with core-shell structure formed in hydrothermal way has a high content of N elements from Aphen, indicating that there is a large amount of bonded Aphen molecules on the surface which endows the ACCDs with certain hydrophobicity. Meanwhile, so as to demonstrate the impact of functionalized Aphen on the chemical structure of CDs resulted from various carbon sources, XPS analyses on AGCDs and CDs-A were performed. Fig. S1 still shows that the elemental compositions of AGCDs and CDs-A are essentially the same as those of ACCDs, but the N atom content remains significantly reduced (Table S1). The formation of the structure is also indirectly demonstrated by the spectroscopic data discussed below. Therefore, we conclude that the luminescent mechanism for CDs is closely dependent to the exposed concentration of N atoms (bonded Aphen structures) on the CDs surface.

The UV–Vis spectra of ACCDs, ACCDs-1, ACCDs-2, AGCDs and CDs-A samples in ethanol (0.1 mg/mL) are shown in Fig. S4. ACCDs has two clear absorption bands at 233 and 273 nm, assigned to CC π-π* transition [57]. Moreover, multiple absorption bands of CN and CO n-π* transitions appear at 350–500 nm (Fig. S4a) [58]. ACCDs-1 has two evident absorptions at 227 and 265 nm, which belong to the CC π-π* transition (Fig. S4b). The absorption peak of ACCDs-2 at 262 nm is the CC π-π* transition. There is a broad absorption band at about 384 nm owing to n-π* transitions of C–O, CO, C–N, and CN bonds (Fig. S4c). AGCDs presents multiple absorption bands of CC π-π* transition at 210–260 nm (Fig. S4d). As shown in Fig. S4e, CDs-A also has multiple absorption bands at 227–273 nm belonging to CC π-π* transition. Simultaneously, the absorption bands at 350–500 nm belong to CN and CO n-π* transition [59]. The UV–Vis spectra show that the absorption bands of ACCDs are stronger and wider than those of the other four CDs, which can trap more excitons for the surface states of the CDs, causing the absorption red-shift owing to the recombination of excitons [60,61]. It implies that the red-shift of characteristic peaks is primarily from the change of Aphen content in the CDs surface state. Hence, the photoluminescence properties of the CDs are dominatingly attributed to the O- and N-containing groups on the CDs surface [52].

As shown in Fig. S5, in order to further catch on the photoluminescence principle of CDs, the fluorescent spectra of five CDs in ethanol solution (0.1 mg/mL) were also recorded. For ACCDs, the emission peak is centered at 430 nm (λex < 400 nm), along with a 500 nm shoulder peak and a diminutive red emission at 640 nm, showing excitation wavelength independence. In addition, the 430 nm emission peak intensity decreases with rising exciting light wavelength. The red-light emission intensity at 640 nm raises gradually with the further increasement of excitation wavelength, and these long emission bands are mainly caused by the structure and surface state of CDs [52]. The typical triple emission peaks of ACCDs with multicore-shell structure originate from three different structures in the CDs, namely the nuclear, edge and surface states. The blue emission at 430 nm comes from the CA-derived multinuclear state. On account of the introduction of Aphen into the CDs, the amino group on Aphen reacts with the carboxyl group on CA to form an amide bond. It is the origin of green light emission at 500 nm, belonging to the edge state of the CDs. While the surface state is from the Aphen part on the surface of ACCDs, which shows red emission at 640 nm in the fluorescence spectra. These Aphen fractions passivate the surface of ACCDs and promote more efficient exciton recombination, leading to a series of emission defects. At the same time, due to the large conjugated rigid sp2 hybrid structure for Aphen on the CDs surface, it also corresponds to the above UV absorption spectra with a large red-shifted emission. Thus, the red-light emission excited at 365 nm can be clearly seen in Fig. 2 [52]. ACCDs-1 displays a representative excitation-dependent feature, mainly located at 360–550 nm, and the emission intensity becomes weaker with raising excitation wavelength. The 640 nm peak intensity does not change with raising exciting wavelength. Since there is only the π-π* transition in UV–Vis spectra (Fig. S5b), the blue-green emission band with low wavelength excitation is concentrated. Aphen was well dissolved in ethanol during the solvothermal reaction, so that Aphen was uniformly bonded to the CDs, resulting in a surface state of ACCDs-1 consisting of both the Aphen part from the CDs surface and the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of CA. The exposed Aphen on the surface of CDs does not have enough excitons for recombination, resulting in UV absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra with shorter wavelength emission compared to ACCDs. ACCDs-2 manifests three emission peaks of 450, 550 and 610 nm as the excitation wavelength raises. In the process of forming CDs, Aphen was dissolved in DMF to obtain evenly distributed CDs, resulting in the surface state of ACCDs-2 containing both hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of CA, as well as uniformly bonded Aphen. Due to the high N atom content of DMF, ACCDs-2 has a higher content of N elements compared to ACCDs-1 (Table S1), the UV absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra have long wavelength emission. The AGCDs show a faint wide emission band of 390–570 nm with increasing excitation wavelength. In the UV absorption spectra of Fig. S5d, there is only an absorption band at 210–260 nm, thus have shorter wavelength emission. The characteristic peaks in the fluorescence spectra of CDs-A are moved to longer wavelengths by degrees. With the exciting wavelength increasing from 300 to 540 nm, a gradually reducing emission intensity at 410 nm was observed, and the intensity of the broad emission band of 500–610 nm first strengthened, then reduced. The emission peak red-shift indicates that CDs-A has an excitation-dependent feature. After CDs-A was grafted with Aphen outside the CACDs, the core-shell like CDs was obtained by carboxyl and amino amidation, which corresponds to the green emission at 500 nm. The red emission at 600 nm is from the surface exposed Aphen part of CDs. By comparing the spectra of ACCDs and CDs-A in ethanol, they have the same emission spectra shape except for the peaks position. Although their preparation methods are diverse, the structure and mechanism of CDs formation should be basically the same. The above data explicitly reveal that the fluorescent feature of different CDs have remarkable distinctions in view of different carbon sources and reaction solvents.

The prepared CDs were dissolved in PVA solution (40 mg/mL) and dropped on glass plates to prepare CDs-PVA composite films, so as to study the RTP properties of CDs in PVA films. For PVA films doped with CDs, RTP is affected by the proportion of precursors for synthesizing CDs. In an effort to confirm the best ratio of precursors, we prepared ACCDs with mass ratio of Aphen/CA at 1:5, 1:9 and 1:15, and doped the as-obtained CDs into PVA with 0.1 mg/mL to evaluate their phosphorescence spectra (Fig. S6a). The different film samples have the strongest 558 nm emission peak as excited at 280 nm, along with a 506 nm shoulder peak. Under 405 nm excitation, the yellow afterglow of 5 s can be caught sight out by naked eyes (Fig. S6b). The RTP intensity of the sample (Aphen/CA = 1:9) is the strongest. ACCDs with different precursor mass ratios have the same UV absorption spectra (Fig. S7). Compared to ACCDs (Aphen/CA = 1:5), the red fluorescence emission at 640 nm is somewhat stronger for ACCDs with an Aphen/CA mass ratio of 1:15, demonstrating the effect of the Aphen content of the CDs surface on the red emission. However, for the effect of phosphorescence in CDs-doped films, excessive Aphen on the CDs surface may aggregate and fail to form efficacious hydrogen bonding with PVA, resulting in enhanced vibration of the system. At the same time, the CDs in this case may also have a self-quenching effect, leading to the reduction of phosphorescence. When the content of Aphen in the CDs surface state is low, there are not enough N atoms in the composite film to promote the n-π* transition and facilitate the ISC. Meanwhile, Aphen cannot generate sufficient hydrogen bonding with PVA to restrain the triplet state non-radiative transition. Therefore, the photophysical nature is closely related to the CDs structure. Thus, we can conclude that the optimal ratio of precursor for ACCDs is 1:9, which synthetic condition will also be used in subsequent studies and the preparation of other CDs.

For ACCDs-PVA films, the chromophore doping concentration directly affects RTP behavior. To determine the optimal doping concentration, luminescence spectra of PVA films with different ACCDs concentrations (0.05, 0.1, and 0.5 mg/mL) were studied (Fig. 4 a and b). Theoretically, RTP will be at the highest when ACCDs at their saturation with the polymer through hydrogen bonding. Too few ACCDs may result in lower RTP intensity on account of lacking RTP centers. Excessive ACCDs can lead to incomplete interactions by aggregation in the PVA matrix affecting RTP performance [62]. The experimental results are very much line with our hypothesis (Fig. 4). As the concentration increases, the luminescence intensity firstly enhances and then declines. Correspondingly, the phosphorescence lifetime (τp) of the three films excited at 280 nm are 326.5 ms, 352.9 ms and 187.4 ms, respectively (Fig. 4c). These results indicate that when the doping concentration is lower than 0.1 mg/mL, the surface of the ACCDs in the film has little N atoms for generating n-π* electron transition to promote ISC, nor enough hydrogen bonding with PVA to restrain the triplet state non-radiative transition. However, adding high concentration (0.5 mg/mL) of ACCDs in the film may also enhances the vibration of the system due to the energy/electron transfer between adjacent ACCDs, resulting in phosphorescence quenching. Therefore, the optimal doping concentration of 0.1 mg/mL was derived and the photoluminescence quantum yield was 36.7%. The following characterizations were all performed using films with CDs content of 0.1 mg/mL. The unique luminescence properties of ACCDs-PVA films should mainly come from the hydrogen bonding interactions produced between ACCDs and the PVA, heightening the rigidity of the phosphor and resulting in the generation of RTP owing to the suppression of triplet state non-radiative transition [57–59,64]. As shown in Fig. S8a, the ACCDs-PVA-0.1 film shows two distinct fluorescence emission peaks under excitation at 280 nm, located at 430 nm and 512 nm, respectively. As the concentration of ACCDs increases, there remains a red-shift at 430 nm blue fluorescence emission, which indicates the increase of CDs agglomeration. The phosphorescence and fluorescence spectra of ACCDs-PVA-0.1 film have excitation independence under excitation at 280–450 nm. Strong visible-light excitation phosphorescence emission of the film can be observed with a small 506 nm shoulder peak and a phosphorescence peak at 552 nm (Fig. S8b). It is noteworthy that the phosphorescence emission peak of ACCDs-PVA-0.1 film has a large red shift as compared with the fluorescence emission peak, which proves the generation of RTP. The single-triplet state energy gap (ΔEST) of ACCDs-PVA-0.1 is obtained by calculating the ΔEST between the single state (S1, 2.88 eV) and triplet state (T1, 2.25 eV) to be 0.63 eV, so the appropriate energy gap from S1 to T1 can efficiently promote the ISC process and generate the RTP emission.

To evaluate the effect of reaction solvent on the CDs structure, ethanol and DMF as solvents were selected to prepare ACCDs-1 and ACCDs-2 via solvothermal method, respectively. The as-prepared CDs were blended with PVA to fabricate CDs-PVA composite films. From the RTP spectra (Fig. S9a) of composite films doped with CDs prepared by different solvents, the phosphorescence emission of ACCDs-1 is at 499 nm and coupled with a small shoulder peak at 470 nm. The phosphorescence emission of ACCDs-2 is at 502 nm. However, compared with ACCDs-PVA-0.1 film, both have a larger blue shift. The films have RTP under ambient conditions after stopping 405 nm light, which can be identified by the human eye for about 2 s (Fig. S9c). It is a typical feature of afterglow materials. Correspondingly, the τp of the two films at 280 nm excitation are 238.1 ms and 240.3 ms, respectively (Fig. S10a-b and Table S2). It is noteworthy that although the RTP phenomenon can be observed in ACCDs-1-PVA and ACCDs-2-PVA films, the phosphorescence intensity of RTP is inferior to that of ACCDs-PVA-0.1. Therefore, we suggest that the surface structure of the CDs prepared by the solvothermal method is different from that of the ACCDs prepared by the hydrothermal method. The N atom amount on the ACCDs surface is higher than that of ACCDs-1 and ACCDs-2, indicating that the CN, C–N and CO structures on the surface of ACCDs and the exposed pyridine N of Aphen have more opportunities to construct hydrogen bonding with the –OH of the PVA matrix, inhibiting the non-radiative transition of the system, facilitating the formation of stable triplet excited states. Moreover, the modulation of the single-triplet energy splitting caused by the n-π* transition contributes to more efficient triplet excitons and facilitates the ISC from S1 to T1, resulting in a longer afterglow [24]. It also confirmed the element content of high-resolution XPS spectra of individual CDs as well as the structure of CDs in Table S1.

To investigate the influence of various carbon sources on the structure of CDs, glucose was selected instead of CA to prepare AGCDs. Fig. S9b depicts the phosphorescence emission spectra of CDs-doped PVA films prepared by different carbon sources, in which AGCDs-PVA film has phosphorescence emission at 485 nm. An afterglow of about 1 s can be observed by the naked eye after stopping the 405 nm illumination (Fig. S9c). The τp of the AGCDs-PVA film is 210.9 ms (Fig. S10c and Table S2), which is typical of phosphorescence materials. We can see that the RTP intensity and the τp of AGCDs-PVA is relatively lower compared to the ACCDs-PVA-0.1. Therefore, we suggest that the surface structures of CDs prepared from different carbon sources are different. The content of N atom on ACCDs is higher than AGCDs (Table S1). The N atom content affects the number of hydrogen bonds formed with the –OH of the PVA matrix, affecting the RTP properties of the films. Next, to demonstrate the important role of Aphen on the structure of CDs and the phosphorescence properties of the films, we prepared pure CA derived CDs (CACDs) and doped them into the PVA matrix at 0.1 mg/mL to investigate the luminescence behavior. As shown in Fig. S11, the CACDs-PVA film has fluorescence emission at room temperature, weak RTP emission under 280 nm and 365 nm excitation, but almost no RTP characteristics under visible light excitation at 405 nm and above. CDs-A was also prepared by post-functionalized grafting of Aphen on CACDs, which was also doped into PVA film. CDs-A-PVA has fluorescence emission and excitation-independent luminescence properties after removing the excitation source, with the green phosphorescence located at 510 nm upon 280–450 nm excitation (Fig. S12a and b). As given in Fig. S10d, Fig. S12c and Table S2, we can observe the green RTP for 3 s with the τp is 240.4 ms. This result proves that the hydrogen bonding interactions are formed between Aphen-exposed pyridine N, C–N, CN and CO structures on the CDs surface and PVA matrix leading to the generation of RTP. A conceivable RTP mechanism for Aphen-based CDs-doped PVA systems can be proposed based on the analysis above. The ACCDs is rich in C–N/CN and CO bonds, and the modulation of the singlet-triplet energy splitting caused by the n-π* transition contributes to more resultful triplet state capture and ISC process from S1 to T1 [63]. More importantly, the pyridine N in a large variety of Aphen molecules exposed in the surface state of the ACCDs can produce hydrogen bonding with the –OH in the PVA. The hydrogen bonds inhibit the non-radiative transition and promote the emergence of steady triplet state, leading to RTP emission [64]. Finally, the Aphen structure is covalently bound to the ACCDs, which further inhibits the molecular vibrations, and the rigid polymer matrix restrains triplet state non-radiative transition, enabling the RTP emission.

To elucidate the origin of RTP emission from CDs/polymer composite films, we prepared different composite films by adding ACCDs to diverse polymer matrices, such as polystyrene (PS) and polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA), and investigated their effects on phosphorescence of CDs. As shown in Fig. S13, the ACCDs in different polymer matrices have different RTP properties. Since there are no –OH in PS and PMMA matrices, strong hydrogen bonding interactions cannot be formed between polymers and ACCDs. As expected, the phosphorescence emission in ACCDs-PMMA and ACCDs-PS films cannot be observed. Compared with PMMA and PS matrices, PVA not only has a polymer rigid structure, but also carries numerous –OH, which can form strong hydrogen bonding interactions. Moreover, the lessened action sites also reduce the possibilities for the generation of hydrogen bonds between polymer films and ACCDs. It is shown that the abundant hydrogen bonding in the system is closely related to the suppression of non-radiative transitions [65].

Compared with hydrogen bonding, covalent bonding is more conducive to the extension of afterglow [47]. Therefore, we have developed ACCDs-BA crystal RTP materials by embedding ACCDs in boric acid (BA) crystal matrix under hydrothermal conditions. Notably, ACCDs were uniformly embedded in the BA crystal. The presence of the rigid BA crystal matrix suppresses the vibrations of the ACCDs and protects the triplet state excitons from quenching, reducing the non-radiative transition of the ACCDs and increasing the ISC. In the fluorescence spectra (Fig. S14a), the maximum fluorescence emission peaks of ACCDs-BA appear at 498, 505, 500 and 507 nm as excited at 280, 365, 405 and 450 nm, respectively. In the phosphorescence spectra (Fig. S14b), ACCDs-BA has a 498 nm emission peak coupled with a small shoulder peak at 558 nm under 280 nm excitation. Compared with the 498 nm emission peak, the phosphorescence emission at 558 nm is enhanced and then diminished with about 14 nm red-shift as the excitation wavelength increases. The photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) for ACCDs-BA composite is 23.7%, and the τp can reach 965.3 ms (Fig. S14c). Notably, intense yellow-orange RTP of ACCDs-BA is visible for up to 13 s by the naked eyes after turning off the 405 nm light source (Fig. S14d). Based on this, the RTP mechanism for ACCDs-BA composites can be proposed. First, as shown inFig. S15 there are O–H, B–O and B–O–H stretching vibrations at 3183, 1414, and 1196 cm−1, while the new characteristic peak at 911 cm−1 assigned to the C–B band [46,47]. This demonstrates the formation of C–B bonds between BA and ACCDs. The p-orbital vacancies on B can attract π-electrons to produce p-π conjugates, which availably reduced free orbital energy level, and the B2O3 glassy state can also defend triplet excitons from quenching, facilitating afterglow emission [66]. Secondly, the inorganic BA matrix has a fixed domain-limiting effect on the encapsulated ACCDs by forming covalent bonding, effectively immobilizing ACCDs and inhibiting the molecular motions [63]. The visible-light-excited RTP behavior further expands the scope of applications for composite materials.

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is considered as a process of non-radiative energy transfer, in which the excited donor fluorophore is able to be coupled to the ground state receptor (another fluorophore or quencher) by dipole-dipole coupling when certain spectral overlap is satisfied [67]. The triplet to singlet energy resonance transfer (TS-FRET) depends on the large spectral overlap of the RTP emission of the donors with the absorption of the acceptors [68]. In recent years, most of the reported TS-FRET systems are organometallic and pure organic molecules [69,70,77,78], and RTP generation by FRET based CDs materials is rarely reported. In 2020, Wang et al. reported the fluorescence FRET between O-CDs and G-CDs, resulting in a significant enhancement of the fluorescence of G-O-CDs [71]. Two years later, their group proposed a strategy to achieve tunable and water-resistant RTP via TS-FRET [72]. Here, we hope to implant TS-FRET into the donor-recipient system to manipulate afterglow color for expanding the RTP behavior of amorphous organic materials (Fig. 5 ). In this work, the existing fluorescent dye RhB as an energy acceptor could be chosen.

As shown in Fig. S16a, there is significant overlap between the RhB absorption spectrum and the phosphorescence spectrum for ACCDs-PVA-0.1 sample, which is a prerequisite for energy transfer from triplet state to singlet state [70]. ACCDs-RhB-PVA's fluorescent spectra show that the fluorescence emission of RhB in the yellow-orange region is red-shifted from 562 nm to 591 nm as the RhB doping mass ratio increases, which results from the FRET process from ACCDs to RhB generating triplet to singlet states (Fig. 5b). The RTP emission spectra for the RhB-doped film (ACCDs-20.0 wt%RhB-PVA) exhibit double emission peaks at 474 and 592 nm (Fig. 5c). The 474 nm fluorescence emission peak is in line with that of ACCDs-PVA, while the emission at 592 nm is originated from the RhB in ethanol solution (Fig. S16b). The RTP emission peak gradually red-shifts with raising RhB dopant mass ratio from 5.0 to 20.0 wt %. According to CIE coordinates, the luminous color of the film changed from yellow to orange-red (Fig. S17). Therefore, CIE coordinates of the ACCDs-20.0 wt% RhB-PVA film is shifted to (0.61, 0.38) after the TS-FRET process, and the optical photographs of the ACCDs-RhB-PVA films are consistent with the CIE coordinate results (Fig. 5d).

Because the RTP polymer material has the advantages of good processability and multi-color afterglow, the polymer film can be cut into the shape of “orange”. As shown in Fig. 6 a, after the 405 nm light irradiation is turned off, we can observe the bright orange afterglow of ACCDs-PVA ink (“orange”) and the green afterglow of CDs-A-PVA ink (“orange leaf”). Furthermore, ACCDs-based composites have unique visible-excited RTP properties, and ACCDs-BA powder can realize information encryption. We employed the decent visible light-responsive RTP characteristics for as-prepared materials to establish a simple encryption model. As shown in Fig. 6b, the numerical pattern “409″ is written with ACCDs-BA powder, and the other pattern is written with Aphen powder to make the numerical pattern “888″ on the black cardboard. Under the excitation of 405 nm, the model pattern is displayed in “888″ orange font, and the orange number” 409″ can be viewed as the 405 nm light is turned off. This is because the ACCDs-BA powder has the RTP characteristic, while the Aphen powder has only the fluorescence feature. Unfortunately, different humidity environments in practical applications will lead to the reduction of its afterglow signal intensity [73]. Therefore, we chose to introduce ACCDs in situ into the epoxy resin curing compound-(EPO) to achieve environmentally stable RTP performance. We doped ACCDs (0.1 mg/mL) into the mixture of epoxy resin and curing agent (W93, an addition product of butyl glycidyl ether and diethylenetriamine), and put it into the flower mold to prepare epoxy-based phosphorescent material denoted as “ACCDs-EPO-0.1". As shown in Fig. 6c, the obtained phosphorescence material can still produce strong phosphorescence under 405 nm excitation after 10 days of immersion in water. It indicates that the cured crosslinked epoxy resin matrix can still form hydrogen bonding with ACCDs, which provides outstanding water resistance and the fixed rigid effect of the crosslinked resin, maintaining the good phosphorescence characteristics derived from ACCDs.

To sum up, we prepared a series of Aphen-based CDs and doped into PVA polymers or cured epoxy resin. On the one hand, the non-radiative transition of triplet excitons with surface state Aphen structure is effectively inhibited by hydrogen bonding and fixed domain-limiting effects. On the other hand, the Aphen structure is covalently bound to the CDs, which further suppresses the vibrations of the molecules and achieved efficient organic RTP. After embedding in the PVA matrix, ACCDs showed visible RTP phenomenon within 5 s, the τp was 352.9 ms, and the maximum luminescence quantum yield was up to 36.7%. The obtained ACCDs-BA composite also exhibited longer RTP, which was visible to the naked eye for about 13 s with a τp of 965.3 ms. At the same time, by using the TS-FRET process, the fluorescent dye RhB was doped and the afterglow color was tuned from yellow to orange. In addition, we have successfully developed good hydrophobic solid phase phosphorescence materials by using ACCDs-EPO. Surprisingly, the RTP phenomenon was still observed for ACCDs-EPO in an aqueous environment for 10 days. Therefore, our tactic starts a new way to expand the existence of RTP materials in the water environment, and greatly extends the potential application scope of organic RTP polymer materials.
